Theme Updates

Managed themes stay in sync with Outpost releases automatically. Your customizations are detected and preserved through a hash-based conflict system.

What are managed themes?

Managed themes are official themes bundled with Outpost and maintained by the Outpost team. They are identified by the "managed": true flag in their theme.json file. The bundled Personal and Starter themes are both managed.

In the admin theme browser (Admin → Themes), managed themes display a small “Managed” badge on their card. This badge indicates that the theme participates in the automatic update system.

Themes you create yourself, or themes you duplicate from a managed original, are not managed and will never be modified by the updater.

How theme updates work

When you update Outpost via Settings → Updates, the update package may include new versions of managed themes. The updater processes them automatically after the core CMS files are updated:

  1. Check for managed themes in the package. The updater looks for theme directories inside the update package that have "managed": true in their theme.json.
  2. Compare against installed themes. For each managed theme in the package:
    • If the theme does not exist on your site, it is installed as a fresh copy.
    • If the theme exists but its theme.json does not have "managed": true, it is skipped entirely. (This handles the case where you duplicated a managed theme and later removed the managed flag.)
    • If the installed version is the same or newer than the package version, it is skipped.
  3. Update with conflict detection. If an update is needed, each file is compared using the .outpost-manifest.json hash file to detect user modifications before replacing anything. See the Conflict Detection section below.
Note — Theme updates happen as part of the CMS update. There is no separate “update themes” button. When you click “Update now” in Settings → Updates, both the core CMS and any managed themes are updated in a single operation.

Conflict detection

The updater is designed to never silently overwrite your changes. It uses a hash-based manifest system to detect which files you have edited.

The .outpost-manifest.json file

Every managed theme ships with a hidden .outpost-manifest.json file at the theme root. This file records the MD5 hash of every file as it was originally shipped:

{
  "index.html": "a1b2c3d4e5f6a1b2c3d4e5f6a1b2c3d4",
  "post.html": "f6e5d4c3b2a1f6e5d4c3b2a1f6e5d4c3",
  "assets/style.css": "1234abcd5678ef901234abcd5678ef90",
  "partials/head.html": "abcd1234efgh5678abcd1234efgh5678"
}

How files are compared

For each file in the update package, the updater makes a three-way comparison:

Installed file matches manifest? Update changes this file? Result
Yes (unmodified) Yes File is replaced with the new version.
Yes (unmodified) No File is replaced (identical content anyway).
No (you edited it) No Your version is kept. No conflict.
No (you edited it) Yes Conflict. Your version is preserved. The file path is reported in the update results.
File does not exist — New file is copied in.

After the update completes, the new .outpost-manifest.json replaces the old one, so future updates compare against the latest shipped version.

What conflicts mean and how to resolve them

A conflict means that both you and the Outpost team changed the same file. The updater preserves your version to avoid losing your work, and reports the file path so you can review it.

Viewing conflicts

After an update, the results screen shows a Theme Updates section. If any themes had conflicts, they are listed with the specific file paths:

  • “Updated (1.0.0 → 2.0.0)” — theme updated cleanly, no conflicts
  • “Updated with conflicts” — theme updated, but some files were preserved. The conflicting paths are shown (e.g. assets/style.css, partials/nav.html).

Resolving conflicts

When you see conflicts, you have a few options:

  1. Do nothing. Your modified files are already in place and working. The rest of the theme was updated around them. This is fine if your changes are still compatible.
  2. Review and merge manually. Open the conflicting files in the Code Editor (Admin → Code Editor) and compare your version against the latest shipped version. You can view the original by checking the Outpost release notes or the GitHub repository.
  3. Accept the new version. If you no longer need your customizations for a specific file, delete it and re-run the update (or manually copy the file from a fresh download).
Tip — Most conflicts happen in assets/style.css where users add custom styles. Consider putting your custom CSS in a separate file and including it via the theme’s head.html partial to avoid future conflicts entirely.

User-created themes are never touched

Any theme without "managed": true in its theme.json is completely invisible to the update system. This includes:

  • Themes you build from scratch
  • Themes you duplicate from a managed theme (the managed flag is stripped automatically on duplicate)
  • Third-party themes you install manually

The updater will never read, modify, or delete files in a non-managed theme, regardless of what the update package contains.

Best practices

  • Duplicate before customizing. If you want to make structural changes to a managed theme (editing templates, adding partials, changing layouts), duplicate it first. The duplicate is yours to edit freely and will never be touched by updates.
  • Use the Theme Customizer for visual tweaks. Colors, fonts, logos, and favicons can be changed via Admin → Customize without editing any files. Customizer values are stored in content/data/customizer.json and survive all updates.
  • Keep custom CSS separate. If you must add CSS to a managed theme, create a new file (e.g. assets/custom.css) and include it in the head.html partial. New files added by you are never overwritten because they do not exist in the update package.
  • Check update results. After every update, review the Theme Updates section to see if any conflicts were reported. Address them promptly to ensure your theme stays compatible with the latest engine changes.
